Hierarchical GAM on fish population trends

Model we are attempting to build:

\[N_{i,t} = f(t, \alpha_i) + g(\lambda_i, \beta_i) + \varepsilon_i\]

Where:

  • \(N\) : population size estimate

  • \(i\) : population

  • \(t\) : time step

  • \(f(t, \alpha_i)\) : function estimating the temporal trend in population size

    • \(\alpha\): estimated parameter describing each population’s temporal slope

  • \(g(\lambda, \beta_i)\) : function estimating latent variation in population size

    • \(\lambda\) : latent variable (to capture covariation between populations)
    • \(\beta\) : estimated parameter describing each population’s latent variation

  • \(\varepsilon\): residual variation

Because the model also estimates latent variation, we are accounting for the covariation between populations when estimating \(N\).

This model then contains key information to calculate and extend an index like the Living Planet Index, to show the average change in population size from a baseline value of 1 in the start year.

The first part of the model estimates the temporal trend of each population’s size (\(\alpha_i\)), which contains information about population-level change and community-level change:

\[\alpha_i \sim N(\mu_{\alpha}, \sigma^2_{\alpha}) \]

where: - {} is the average population trend - ^2{} is the variance in this average trend, giving us a measure of how variable the population trends are within the sample.

To calculate the index of change from a baseline of 1 (as in the LPI), the average population trend can then be plugged in to the LPI calculation as follows:

\[ I_t = {\mu_{\alpha}}*t + 1 \]

This trend can then be visualized and communicated with its known (\(\sigma^2_{\alpha}\)).

#### import & set up data ####

# from https://github.com/eric-pedersen/groundfish-data-analysis
# note: these are mean biomasses over the whole sampled area with an SE
# not actually raw abundances. But for this purpose, should be ok
load("~/Documents/GitHub/groundfish-data-analysis/data/year_geom_means.Rdata")

time = rownames(Year_Geom_Means_all) %>% as.numeric()

# get number of populations & length of time series
npops <- ncol(Year_Geom_Means_all)
tsl <- nrow(Year_Geom_Means_all)

Building the model with mgcv

# scale all columns
Year_Geom_Means_all = apply(Year_Geom_Means_all, 2, scale) %>% as.data.frame()
Year_Geom_Means_all$time = as.integer(time) 
df = Year_Geom_Means_all %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -time, 
               names_to = "species", "values_to" = "N")
df$species <- factor(df$species)

# build a GI model: global smoother plus group-level smoothers with differing wiggliness

m <- gam(N ~ # global smooth
           s(time, bs = c("tp"), m = 2, k = 30) +
           # smooth per species
           s(time, by = species, bs = c("tp"), m = 1, k = 30) +
           # random intercept
           s(species, bs = c("re"), k = 30),
         data = df,
         method="REML",
         family="gaussian")

# save model
saveRDS(m, here("models/hgam.RDS"))

Model performance

gam.check(m)

## 
## Method: REML   Optimizer: outer newton
## full convergence after 12 iterations.
## Gradient range [-8.007026e-05,0.0002563674]
## (score 847.2227 & scale 0.127037).
## Hessian positive definite, eigenvalue range [5.860272e-06,497.7371].
## Model rank =  930 / 930 
## 
## Basis dimension (k) checking results. Low p-value (k-index<1) may
## indicate that k is too low, especially if edf is close to k'.
## 
##                                                      k'      edf k-index

summary(m)
## 
## Family: gaussian 
## Link function: identity 
## 
## Formula:
## N ~ s(time, bs = c("tp"), m = 2, k = 30) + s(time, by = species, 
##     bs = c("tp"), m = 1, k = 30) + s(species, bs = c("re"), k = 30)

##               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)

##

## -REML = 847.22  Scale est. = 0.12704   n = 990

Predicting the full model

Predict global smooth (overall temporal trend)

# plot the global smooth s(time)
plot(m, 
     residuals = TRUE, shade = TRUE, shade.col = "lightblue", 
     select = 1, 
     seWithMean = TRUE, 
     shift = unname(coef(m)[1]))

Predict species smooths

# Evaluate the smooth over a range of values for `year`
time_smest <- smooth_estimates(m, "s(time)", n = tsl)
# add a confidence interval
time_sm <- confint(m, "s(time)", n = tsl)
## Warning: `evaluate_smooth()` was deprecated in gratia 0.7.0.
## ℹ Please use `smooth_estimates()` instead.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the gratia package.
##   Please report the issue at <]8;;https://github.com/gavinsimpson/gratia/issueshttps://github.com/gavinsimpson/gratia/issues]8;;>.
ggplot(data = time_sm, aes(x = time)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper, fill = smooth), alpha = .1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = est, col = smooth)) +
  geom_ribbon(data = filter(time_sm, smooth == "s(time)"),
              aes(x = time, ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = .4) +
  geom_line(data = filter(time_sm, smooth == "s(time)"),
            aes(x = time, y = est)) +
  labs(y = "s(time)", x = "") +
  ggpubr::theme_pubr() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Extracting model coefficients: alpha

time_smest
## # A tibble: 33 × 6
##    smooth  type  by       est     se  time
##    <chr>   <chr> <chr>  <dbl>  <dbl> <int>
##  1 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  2.22   0.0902  1981
##  2 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  2.18   0.0682  1982
##  3 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  2.10   0.0657  1983
##  4 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  1.84   0.0642  1984
##  5 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  1.39   0.0636  1985
##  6 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  0.986  0.0634  1986
##  7 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  0.700  0.0633  1987
##  8 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  0.513  0.0632  1988
##  9 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  0.330  0.0632  1989
## 10 s(time) TPRS  <NA>  0.0979 0.0632  1990
## # … with 23 more rows
plot(x = time_smest$time, y = time_smest$est, type = "l")
lines(x = time_smest$time, 
      y = time_smest$est + time_smest$se, lty = 2)
lines(x = time_smest$time, 
      y = time_smest$est - time_smest$se, lty = 2)

Extract covariance between populations

Vb <- vcov(m) # default is the bayesian covariance matrix
heatmap(Vb, Rowv = NA, Colv = NA)

# Vb_sponly <- Vb[which(grepl("species", rownames(Vb))),
#                 which(grepl("species", colnames(Vb)))]

#   Vb_sponly_ls[[i]] <- Vb_sponly[
#     which(grepl(paste0(i), rownames(Vb_sponly))),
#     which(grepl(paste0(i), colnames(Vb_sponly)))]
# }

# corrplot::corrplot(Vb_sponly_ls[[i]], 
#                    method = "color", 
#                    type = "lower", 
#                    tl.pos = "n")
# }

Extract global population trend

Note: this is just the temporal trend, without the latent variation or the residual error.

Average slope of the temporal trend in population size (LPI-pop)

lpipop = filter(time_sm, smooth == "s(time)")$est

Variance in the temporal trend across populations (LPI-comm)

lpicomm = filter(time_sm, smooth == "s(time)")$se
# not sure about this one...
# drawing posteriors for the predicted response values:

beta <- coef(m)   # vector of model parameters

Vb <- vcov(m)     # default is the bayesian covariance matrix
heatmap(Vb, Rowv = NA, Colv = NA)

# sample from the posterior distribution
m_post <- smooth_samples(m, 's(time)', n = 100, seed = 42) 

post_summary <- m_post %>% 
  group_by(.x1) %>% 
  summarise(mu = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            lower = quantile(value, 0.025),
            upper = quantile(value, 0.975),
            variance = var(value)) %>% ungroup()

time_fit <- evaluate_smooth(m, 's(time)', n = tsl)

draw(time_fit) + 
  geom_line(data = m_post, 
            aes(x = .x1, y = value, group = draw),
            alpha = 0.3, colour = 'red')
